words.exampleNL: Ik ga naar de bank om een rekening te openen.
words.exampleEN: I am going to the bank to open an account.
words.audioHint: Similar to English 'bank'. The 'a' is short. Also means 'couch'!

words.exampleEN: I want to open a bank account.
words.audioHint: Three syllables. Also means 'bill' at a restaurant.

words.exampleNL: Kan ik met mijn pinpas betalen?
words.exampleEN: Can I pay with my debit card?
words.audioHint: 'pin' from PIN code + 'pas' (card). Very common in the Netherlands.
Stress on the second syllable. Long 'a' sound.

words.exampleNL: Ik maak het geld vandaag over.
words.exampleEN: I will transfer the money today.
words.audioHint: Separable verb: 'over' + 'maken'. In sentences, 'over' goes to the end.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb niet genoeg geld bij me.
words.exampleEN: I don't have enough money with me.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. Short 'e' sound. The 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Wat is het saldo op mijn rekening?
words.exampleEN: What is the balance on my account?
words.audioHint: Italian origin. Stress on the first syllable.

words.exampleNL: Ik spaar elke maand een beetje geld.
words.exampleEN: I save a little money every month.
words.audioHint: Long 'a' sound. The 'sp' is pronounced as in English.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb geen contant geld, alleen een pinpas.
words.exampleEN: I have no cash, only a debit card.
words.audioHint: Two words. 'contant' has stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: De rente op de spaarrekening is laag.
words.exampleEN: The interest on the savings account is low.
words.audioHint: Two syllables. Short 'e' in both. The final 'e' is a schwa.
Compound word: 'rekening' + 'nummer'. In NL, this is often an IBAN.

words.exampleEN: I want to withdraw one hundred euros.
words.audioHint: Separable verb: 'op' + 'nemen'. Also means 'to pick up (the phone)'.
